segments.s544-g1: کنون (an adverb) + بشنو (imperative) opens with a temporal adverb 'now' followed by the command 'listen'. از introduces the object of بشنو and here marks the topic 'the tale concerning'; دخت مهرک is a genitival phrase 'the daughter of Mehrak' (Mehrak is a proper name). سخن is the noun 'speech/tale' that functions as the object introduced by از; the order is adverb → verb → prepositional object (poetic word order).
segments.s544-g2: This short phrase is a name/epithet cluster: ابا گرد appears here as part of a proper name or epithet attached to شاپور; شمشیرزن is a postpositive appellation 'the swordsman' describing Shapur. In Persian poetry epithets often follow names without linking particles, so read this as 'Aba-gird Shapur, the swordsman.'
segments.s544-g3: چو introduces a temporal clause here meaning 'when'. لختی is an adverbial duration 'for a short while'. برآمد is the past intransitive 'arose/appeared'. برین روزگار is an archaic demonstrative noun phrase 'this/that age' written here as the compound بر + ین (poetic spelling) meaning 'that/this time.'
segments.s544-g4: فروزنده شد is a verbal-predicative sequence: فروzende (radiant, literally 'illuminating') + شد ('became') = 'became radiant'. دولت شهریار is a noun phrase 'the sovereign's/state's fortune' where the ezāfe is omitted in poetic register; the verb precedes the noun phrase in this poetic order but semantically reads 'the sovereign's fortune became radiant.'
segments.s544-g5: به نخچیر is a purpose/goal phrase 'to (the) hunt' (به + نخچیر). شد is the past auxiliary/lexical verb marking the event 'went/occurred' (poetic usage here: 'went (to hunt)'). شاه is the subject 'the king'. روزی پگاه is a temporal adverbial 'one morning at dawn' (روزی = one day/one morning; پگاه = dawn). The clause shows inverted/poetic ordering but means 'the king went hunting one morning at dawn.'
segments.s544-g6: خردمند شاپور is a nominal phrase with an adjective preceding the name: 'the wise Shapur.' با او is the accompaniment 'with him'. به راه is an idiomatic locative/ directional phrase 'on the road / along the way' indicating that Shapur accompanied the king as they set out. Word order is adjective + name, then prepositional phrases indicating accompaniment and direction.
segments.s544-g7: به هر سو is a directional phrase 'to every side / in every direction.' سواران is the subject 'the horsemen/riders.' همی is an archaic adverbial particle indicating continuous or emphatic action ('kept on / indeed'). تاختند is the past plural of تازدن 'to rush/gallop/charge.' The clause therefore means 'the riders galloped off in every direction.'
segments.s544-g8: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' نخچیر here names the hunting expedition or the game (the source/event they depart from). دشتی is a place/land noun 'plain' (used adjectivally as a place name or destination in poetry). بپرداختند in this context is an archaic verbal use meaning 'they set off/rode away' (not the ordinary modern senses 'paid' or 'attended to'); the verb therefore indicates their departure from the hunt into the plain.
segments.s544-g57: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed poetic ordering = "thus gave (an) answer"; داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' (3rd person) and پاسخ is its object 'answer/reply'. The particle که introduces the reported content clause that follows ("that / that this …"). این داستان = "this tale/this story," with این as the demonstrative modifying داستان.
segments.s544-g58: شنیدم is 1st-person past 'I heard'. بسی is an adverb meaning 'much/often' (classical Persian use) qualifying شنیدم. از لب راستان is a prepositional phrase 'from the lips of the righteous', where از marks source, لب='lip(s)', and راستان functions as a substantive 'the righteous' (people) whose lips are the source of the tale.
segments.s544-g59: که begins a relative/reporting clause 'that…'. شاپور is the clause subject. گردست here refers to the arm/wrist (poetic body-part term); با + زور + پیل expresses an instrumental/qualitative phrase 'with the strength of an elephant' (با marks the accompanying quality, زور = 'strength', پیل = 'elephant' as the simile source).
segments.s544-g60: به بخشندگی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in generosity' or 'with regard to generosity' (به used adverbially). همچو دریای نیل is a simile 'like the sea (of) the Nile' where همچو = 'like' and دریای نیل = 'the Nile (river)'. The copula ('is') is omitted in poetic style and is understood from context.
segments.s544-g61: به بالای سروست is a locative/comparative phrase 'is above/higher than the cypress'; note the poetic -ست (an older form of است) marking the 3rd‑person copula attached to سرو. و connects the two predicates. رویین‌تنست is a compound adjective 'iron-skinned / armored' with the same -ست copula; together the two predicates describe his stature and invulnerability.
segments.s544-g62: به هرچیز ماننده = 'in every thing / in all respects similar to'; هرچیز 'everything/anything' and ماننده 'like/resembling'. بهمنست uses the archaic copular suffix -ست again: the sense is 'is like Bahman' (i.e. he resembles Bahman), so the whole clause says he resembles Bahman in all things. The copula is fused into the final word as typical in classical style.
segments.s544-g63: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to her/to him' (dative 'to her'), and گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (poetic order places the indirect object before the verb). شاپور is the speaker; کای is a vocative particle 'O' (an address), and ماه‌روی is the vocative epithet 'moon-faced' (beautiful-faced).
segments.s544-g64: سخن هرچ پرسم is a subordinate content/relative idea 'whatever words/questions I ask' — سخن 'speech/words', هرچ 'whatever', and پرسم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive-like 'I ask' (classical usage giving the sense 'may I ask / I will ask'). ترا is the object 'you' realized as the clitic ت plus object marker را (ت + را), and راست‌گوی is an address/adjective 'truth-speaking' = 'speak truthfully' (imperative/characteristic); overall: 'whatever I ask you, be truthful.'
segments.s544-g33: پرستنده (subject) + بشنید (past verb) states who heard. The conjunction و links the next clause; آمد is the simple past 'came' and دوان is an adverb/participle 'running/rushing' that follows آمد to give 'came running' — a common poetic sequence where a motion verb is followed by an adverbial form.
segments.s544-g34: رسن is the rope (object) followed by برد here used as the past of بردن 'to take/seize' (the provided senses did not include this verbal sense, so its dictionary senses do not match). بر introduces a locative/placement phrase 'upon/on', چرخ is 'wheel' (here a pulley/wheel), and دلو گران is a noun + adjective combination 'the heavy bucket' (poetic order). The whole phrase means he seized the rope and placed the heavy bucket on the wheel.
segments.s544-g35: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. دلو is 'bucket' modified by the compound adjective گران‌سنگ 'very heavy/weighty'. پر here is the predicative 'full (of)', آب is 'water', and گشت is the past of گشتن 'became' — 'became full of water.'
segments.s544-g36: پرستنده را uses را as the marker of the affected person ('the worshipper'). روی here means 'on the face / face-first' and پرتاب گشت is a resultative/passive-like expression: پرتاب (a noun 'throw') with گشت ('became') yields 'was thrown' or 'was flung' — i.e. 'the worshipper was flung face-first.'
segments.s544-g37: چو 'when' introduces the clause. دلو گران 'the heavy bucket' is the subject/topic; برنیامد is the negative past of برآمدن 'did not come up' (here 'would not come up' from the well). ز is the poetic form of 'from', and چاه is 'well' — 'when the heavy bucket did not come up from the well.'
segments.s544-g38: بیامد is the past 'came/arrived'. ژکان is an obscure/dialectal noun here (meaning uncertain in this context — likely some attendants or a group) so its precise sense is unclear. زود 'quickly/soon' modifies the coming, and شاپور شاه (name + title) appears at the end as the subject/agent 'King Shapur' — a common poetic ordering (verb before subject).
segments.s544-g39: پرستنده را shows the indirect object ('to the worshipper'), with را marking the person affected. گفت is the speech verb 'said' (the provided dictionary senses did not list this conjugated verb sense, so it does not match exactly). کای is the vocative 'O', and نیم‌زن is an epithet 'half‑woman' (literally 'half-woman', used here as an address).
segments.s544-g40: نه here functions as negation 'not'. زن is 'woman', and داشت is the past of داشتن 'had/possessed' — so 'was not for a woman' or 'did not belong to a woman' in idiomatic reading. این دلو 'this bucket' is the thing possessed/being talked about; و چندین رسن 'and many ropes' lists additional related items (چندین = 'several/many', رسن = 'ropes'). Overall the clause conveys the king's rebuke that the bucket and its many ropes are not for a woman to handle.
segments.s544-g89: به دو گفت = 'he said to them/him'. به is the dative preposition 'to'; دو here is an archaic/object pronoun ('him' or 'them') so the prepositional phrase marks the indirect object. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (verb governs the following reported noun phrase). کین دختر خوب‌چهر is a noun phrase 'that fair‑faced daughter/maiden' (کین is an archaic demonstrative 'that', followed by the head noun دختر and the adjectival epithet خوب‌چهر). The whole segment supplies the words that were said (direct object of گفت).
segments.s544-g90: به من ده = imperative 'give (her) to me' — به من marks the beneficiary/indirect object ('to me'); the verb ده is an imperative of دادن ('give') understood here as carrying the direct object (the previously mentioned daughter). بر من گوا کن سپهر = 'make the heavens witness upon me' or 'let heaven be my witness': بر من ('upon me') marks the person concerned, گوا is 'witness' used as verbal/noun in an idiom, and کن is the causative/auxiliary 'do/make' (imperative) that forms 'make (X) witness'. سپهر (heaven) is the thing invoked as witness. The two clauses together form a single imperative plea: give her to me and swear (by the heavens) on my behalf.
segments.s544-g91: بدو داد مهتر به فرمان اوی = 'the elder gave (her) to him at his command'. Poetic word order places the indirect object (بدو 'to him') before the verb; داد is the past tense 'gave'; مهتر ('the elder/chief') is the agent (subject). به فرمان اوی is a prepositional phrase 'by his command' (به فرمان = 'by/at the order', اوی = possessive 'his'), specifying that the action was done on someone's instruction.
segments.s544-g92: بر آیین آتش‌پرستان اوی = 'in accordance with the ritual/custom of his fire‑worshippers'. بر ('upon/on') with آیین ('ritual, practice') yields the idiom 'بر آیین' meaning 'according to the rite/custom of'; آتش‌پرستان is the group 'fire‑worshippers'; اوی is the possessive 'his', attaching to the preceding noun phrase to mean 'his (fire‑worshippers)'. This phrase qualifies how the handover was carried out.
segments.s544-g25: بدو (be + -o) is a clitic prepositional phrase meaning “to her/him/it” placed before the verb. گفت is the past verb “said.” شاپور is the subject (name) after the verb in this inverted poetic word order. کای is an archaic vocative particle (“O”), and ماه‌روی is a compound adjective “moon‑faced/beautiful‑faced” addressing the addressee.
segments.s544-g26: چرا is the question word “why.” رنجه گشتی is a light‑verb construction where رنجه (past/participle) + گشت(ی) expresses “become distressed / was troubled” (i.e. you have been troubled). بدین گفت‌وگوی = بدین (by/with this) + گفت‌وگوی (this conversation) marks the cause/means: “by this talk.”
segments.s544-g27: که introduces a subordinate clause meaning “that / so that / who.” باشند is a subjunctive/optative present form “may be / let there be / be (they).” با من means “with me.” پرستنده مرد is a noun phrase “devoted man(s) / worshipper(s)” (پرستنده modifying مرد).
segments.s544-g28: کزین = ک+ این (archaic “from this / of this”). چاه is “well.” بی‌بن is an adjective “without base/bottom” (here ‘bottomless’). کشند is the present/subjunctive plural of کشیدن “to draw/pull (up)” governing the object آب سرد “cold water”; the verb comes before its object in this clause.
segments.s544-g29: ز is the poetic preposition “from.” برنا کنیزک is a noun phrase with the adjective برنا (“youthful, young”) preceding the diminutive noun کنیزک (“young maidservant”). بپیچید is the past tense “she turned” (ب‑ + پیچید), and روی is the direct object “(her) face” — together “she turned her face.”
segments.s544-g30: بشد دور literally “it became distant / she became far,” here idiomatically “she withdrew / went away.” و is the coordinating “and.” بنشست is the past “she sat down.” بر پیش جوی = بر (on/upon) + پیش (before/in front of) + جوی (stream), so she sat down in front of/by the stream.
segments.s544-g31: پرستنده‌ای را is the indefinite object marked by را: “a devotee/worshipper.” بفرمود (ب + فرمود) is the honorific/polite past “he commanded/ordered/said,” and شاه (king) is the subject placed last in this inverted poetic order: literally “a devotee he commanded — the king.”
segments.s544-g32: که introduces the content of the command: دلو آور is an imperative sequence “bring the bucket” (دلو = bucket, آور = bring). و connects the two orders, and آب برکش is “and draw/pull up the water” (آب = water; برکش here is imperative ‘pull up / draw’). ز چاه is the prepositional phrase “from the well.”
segments.s544-g9: پدید آمد is a two-word verbal phrase meaning ‘appeared’ or ‘came into view’ (پدید 'appear' + آمد 'came'). It is followed by the adverbial از دور 'from afar' and then the noun phrase دشتی فراخ (دشتی = 'a plain', فراخ = 'spacious/wide'), so the order is verb → location/adverbial → described noun (poetic word order where the event is stated first and the place is specified after).
segments.s544-g10: پرِ از is a prepositional/participle-like phrase meaning 'full of' (پر 'full' + از 'of'). It lists a series of nouns joined by و (and). This whole segment modifies the preceding دشتی فراخ: 'a broad plain, full of ...'. The items are simple nouns enumerated in coordination.
segments.s544-g11: همی تاخت is a habitual/continuous past construction: همی (still/kept) + تاخت (galloped/rode hard). The subject name شاپور follows the verb (poetic freedom of order). تا + پیش ده together mean 'up to/in front of the village' (تا = 'until/as far as'; پیش = 'in front of / before'; ده = 'village' understood from context).
segments.s544-g12: فرود آمد is a past intransitive verb meaning 'alighted' or 'dismounted' (فرود 'descent/alighting' + آمد 'came'). از راه is an adverbial phrase 'from/on the road'; در here functions as the noun 'door/gate' (درِ خانِ مه = 'the door/gate of the khan/inn'), so در خان مه is a genitival construction 'at the inn/house of the great khan'. The sequence gives the place and manner of dismounting.
segments.s544-g13: یکی introduces an indefinite/particular item 'a certain' or 'one'. باغ بد کش and خرم سرای are two coordinated noun phrases describing the place: باغ بد کش is an idiomatic compound (بد + کش) giving the sense of a far‑stretching/expansive garden, and خرم سرای is 'a joyful/pleasant abode' (خرم modifying سرای). The phrase functions as an appositive description of the place encountered.
segments.s544-g14: جوان is the subject (here 'a young man') and اندر آمد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'entered' or 'came in' (اندر = 'in/within' + آمد = 'came'). بدان is the demonstrative 'to/into that' (here segmented as ب + دان in the word list), and سبز جای = 'green place' (سبز modifying جای). The clause therefore says that a young man entered that green spot.
segments.s544-g15: یکی introduces 'a certain' and دختری is the direct object 'a girl/maiden'. دید is the past verb 'saw' with an understood subject (the young man from the previous clause). بر سان ماه is a simile: بر + سان = 'in the manner of / like', ماه = 'the moon' (a common poetic metaphor for beauty), so 'a girl like the moon.'
segments.s544-g16: فروهشته is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'cast down/let fall' or 'fallen' and here qualifies the girl; از چرخ 'from the sky/heaven' gives the origin ('cast down from the heavens'). دلوی is an adjectival phrase 'kind‑hearted / tender‑hearted' describing her disposition, and به چاه is a locative phrase 'by/at the well'. The sequence piles descriptive phrases after the noun in typical poetic compression.
segments.s544-g65: پدیدار کن is an imperative phrase: پدیدار (make manifest / reveal) + کن (do! — imperative). تا here introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'), and نژاد تو چیست is an embedded question/nominal clause ('what is your lineage?'). چی‌ست is the classical split of 'چی است' (what + is) forming the copular question.
segments.s544-g66: برین is the preposition بر ('on/upon') fused with the demonstrative این ('this') and precedes the noun (classical order). چهره تو is the possessive noun phrase 'your face', and نشان is 'sign/token'. کییست is the older orthography of 'کی است' ('who/what is it?'), forming the final question about the identity/ownership of that sign.
segments.s544-g67: بدو گفت is a classical sequence: بدو ('to him') comes before the reporting verb گفت ('said') — the indirect object precedes the verb. من دختر مهترم is the reported clause: a nominal predicate 'I (am) the daughter of the chief' (دختر + مهترم expresses the genitive/possessive relation in compact classical phrasing).
segments.s544-g68: ازیرا here functions as an archaic connective ('from this / therefore / for that reason') introducing the speaker's claim; چنین = 'thus / so', خوب = 'beautiful / fair' (an adjectival predicate), و = 'and', and کنداورم is a verb/nominal with a first-person suffix -م indicating the speaker (literal etymology unclear in this listing), so the clause means roughly 'therefore I am thus fair and ...' with the final word as the speaker's self-description.
segments.s544-g69: چنین داد پاسخ is a past‑tense report: چنین ('thus') + داد ('gave') + پاسخ ('answer') — 'she gave such an answer'. که introduces the content clause (that...), and هرگز دروغ places the negator 'never' before 'lie' to mean 'never a lie'.
segments.s544-g70: This subordinate clause states the result: بر شهریاران = 'upon/to the kings' (بر + plural 'شهریاران'), نگیرد is a present/subjunctive-style verb meaning 'does not take/seize' (i.e. 'does not attach' or 'does not cling'), and فروغ ('radiance/splendour') names what would be affected; syntactically the object/affected thing is placed after the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s544-g71: کشاورز را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker ('as for a farmer' / 'a farmer ...'), followed by the noun phrase دختر ماه‌روی ('a moon‑faced daughter' = a fair/beautiful maiden). The phrase sets up the subject/topic that the following predicate will comment on.
segments.s544-g72: نباشد is a negative copular/existential in classical Persian ('there is not' / 'is not the case'); بدین is a contraction of به + این ('with / in this'), modifying روی ('face'). و این رنگ و بوی lists further attributes ('and this colour/complexion and scent'). Overall the predicate denies that a farmer's daughter bears such a face, complexion and scent.
segments.s544-g81: This clause is the reporting clause: subject کنیزک (the little maid) + indirect object بدو (to him) precede the verb گفت (said), following Persian SOV word order. کز is the archaic/poetic form of که + از or the conjunction 'that' introducing the reported speech that follows; راه داد here is a fused, poetic noun phrase (راهِ داد) meaning 'the path/way of justice' (راه + داد 'justice'), functioning as part of the subordinate content (the phrasing is compressed in poetry).
segments.s544-g82: This is the reported speech proper. منم is the clitic copula (من + ـم) meaning 'I am' (the linking 'am' is fused onto the pronoun). دختر مهرک is a noun phrase 'the daughter of Mehrak' with the ezafe (دخترِ مهرک) omitted as is common in epic poetry; مهرک here is a proper name. نوش‌زاد is an epithet/adjective 'newly-born' modifying the noun phrase.
segments.s544-g83: The clause is inverted: پارسایی (piety) is the subject, بیاورد is a 3rd-person past/present-form of آوردن meaning 'brought' or 'caused to come', خرد is the direct object 'wisdom', and مرا (here split as م + را in the word list) is the object marker/indirect object 'me' ('piety brought me wisdom'). The verb گَشت (become) is not present here; meaning is literal 'piety brought me wisdom'.
segments.s544-g84: بدین is a poetic fusion of به + این = 'to this'; پرهنر مهتر is a noun phrase 'this cultured/distinguished chief' (پرهنر modifying مهتر). سپرد is the past of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over' and takes the earlier مرا as its object: 'he entrusted me to this skilled chief.' Note the compressed, archaic spacing and omission of an explicit pronoun before سپرد is typical in epic diction.
segments.s544-g85: من is the subject 'I'. از بیم is a causal prepositional phrase 'out of/from fear' (از used in the sense 'because of'), followed by آن نامور شهریار 'that famed king' as the object of the fear. The whole phrase gives the reason: 'because of fear of that renowned king.'
segments.s544-g86: چنین 'thus/so' introduces the resulting state. آبکش here is used metaphorically ('colander/strainer') as a pejorative name for a lowly servant; گشتم is the past of شدن 'I became' (گشتم = 'I became'). و پیشکار appends another role 'and an attendant/servant.' The verb گشتم governs the predicates that follow in this compressed poetic line.
segments.s544-g87: بیامد is a simple past 'came.' بپردخت is an archaic/poetic past form of پرداختن (here meaning 'advanced/turned toward' or 'paid homage/approached') attached to the name that follows: 'بپرداخت شاپور' = 'Shapur advanced/approached.' جای is 'place/position' and marks that Shapur took up his place.
segments.s544-g88: همی بود uses the progressive/continuous past marker همی + بود 'was (standing)' to set the scene. مهتر 'the chief' is the subject; به پیشش 'in front of him / before him' and به پای 'at (his) feet' are prepositional phrases giving the chief's position — a typical courtly image of someone prostrating or standing low before a lord. Word order is somewhat inverted and compressed by the poetic diction.
segments.s544-g49: چو functions here as a temporal conjunction 'when'; the clause order is subject (کنیزک) + temporal marker (چو) + pronoun (او) + object marked by را (دلو را) + past verb برکشید (3rd person singular). را is the definite‑object marker, and برکشید is the classical past form 'lifted/raised'.
segments.s544-g50: بیامد is past 'came' (3rd singular). به here marks manner ('by/with') and governs the following phrase; مهر آفرین is a collocation meaning affectionate praise/kind acclamation, and گسترید is the past 'spread/extended' (3rd singular). The clause means she came, spreading/uttering affectionate praise.
segments.s544-g51: که introduces this clause (here introducing the content of the wish). نوشه is a noun 'a draught/sip' and بدی is used adjectivally/nominatively as 'evil/bad', so نوشه بدی = 'an evil draught'. تا ... بود روزگار uses تا 'until/as long as' plus بود 'be/is' with روزگار 'time/age' to mean 'as long as time endures'.
segments.s544-g52: همیشه 'always' sets the adverbial frame. خرد 'wisdom' is the subject/thing wished for. بادت is the optative construction: باد (optative 'may it be') + enclitic -ت 'to you' = 'may ... be to you'. آموزگار is the predicate noun 'teacher/guide'. Together: 'May wisdom always be your teacher.'
segments.s544-g53: به نیروی is a prepositional phrase 'by/through the power of'; نیروی is the izafet/construct form of 'power/force'. شاپور شاه اردشیر is a sequence of names/titles in apposition (classical word order allows stacked names/titles) — 'Shapur, the king Ardashir' / 'by Shapur the king (to) Ardashir' depending on context, but grammatically it's a name phrase governed by به نیروی.
segments.s544-g54: شود is the classical 3rd singular form of شدن 'become' (here future/predictive). بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly'. The noun phrase آب در چاه شیر is a verbless clause where شیر (here 'milk') is understood as the predicate: 'the water in the well (will be) milk' — a poetic, elliptical prediction of abundance.
segments.s544-g55: جوان is the agent 'the young man'. گفت is the past tense 'said' (here used as simple past speech verb). با introduces the person spoken to ('to/with'); دختر is 'girl/maiden' and the following compound adjective چرب‌گوی ('smooth‑tongued' / flattering) modifies دختر. Word order places the adjective after the noun, common in classical diction.
segments.s544-g56: چه دانی is an interrogative: چه 'what' + دانی 'you know' (2nd person present), producing 'what do you know'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that I am Shapur'; شاپورم is the name with the 1st‑person suffix -م, an archaic/poetic way to render the copular 'I am Shapur'. ای ماه‌روی is a vocative 'O moon‑faced (beautiful) one.'
segments.s544-g41: همی before the verb is an archaic/poetic iterative/progressive marker meaning 'kept on' or 'continually'. برکشید is a past 3rd-person singular verb 'lifted/drew up' (here of water). The object phrase آب چندین ز چاه is literal: آب (water) modified by چندین ('several/many') with ز (poetic form of از) marking source 'from the well' (چاه). Word order is somewhat inverted/compact as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s544-g42: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you' (addressing some referent in the poem). گشتی here is a past form of گشتن used to mean 'became'/'turned' (past 2nd person in surface form) — in older/poetic Persian گشت/گشتی can render 'you became'. پر از is a fixed copular/ adjectival pattern 'full of', where از marks the content; رنج و فریادخواه are the coordinated nouns 'suffering and one who cries for help'.
segments.s544-g43: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. رسن is the noun 'rope/rein' and functions as the subject/agent in this short clause (verb–subject order is acceptable in classical style). بستد is past 3rd-person singular 'tied/bound'. از پیشکار is a prepositional phrase with از marking relation to the attendant (here roughly 'involving/with the attendant' or 'from the attendant'); the line is compact/elliptical about who was tied or what was tied to whom.
segments.s544-g44: شد is the past of 'to become'/'to happen' (here 'became' or 'fell upon'). آن کار دشوار is the nominal subject phrase 'that difficult task/affair'. بر introduces the target/recipient of the happening ('upon' or 'on') and شاه خوار is a noun phrase where خوار postmodifies شاه to mean 'the despised/lowly king' or 'the king, despised'; together the phrase says the difficult affair befell or weighed upon the (despised) king.
segments.s544-g45: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'/'because of' introducing the source or cause ('from the heavy bucket'). دلو گران is a noun phrase 'the heavy/precious bucket' (گران qualifying دلو). شاه is the subject 'the king'. چون ... دید is a classical temporal/subordinating structure 'when/since he saw' with دید as past 3rd-person 'he saw'. The clause therefore reads 'When the king saw the suffering from the heavy bucket...'.
segments.s544-g46: بر آن is the prepositional target 'upon that (one)'; خوب‌رخ is the noun/adjective 'fair‑faced' or 'beautiful-faced' (the person being praised). آفرین here functions as the noun/exclamation 'praise/acclamation' (not the verbal root 'to create'), and گسترید is past 3rd-person 'spread'/'uttered' — together آفرین گسترید is an idiom 'he uttered/poured forth praise'.
segments.s544-g47: که introduces a content/relative clause 'that/which'. برتافت is past 3rd‑person 'he endured' or 'he bore'. دلوی modifies the agent and means 'kind‑hearted' or 'generous‑hearted'. برین سان is an archaic phrase meaning 'in this/that manner' (بر + ین (variant of 'این') + سان 'such'), so برین سان گران means 'so heavy'/'thus heavy'; گران is the adjective 'heavy/weighty' (here figuratively 'burdensome').
segments.s544-g48: همانا is an emphatic 'verily'/'indeed'. که here introduces the following copular clause. هست is the copula 'is'. از نژاد سران is a prepositional phrase 'of the lineage/stock of chiefs' (از = 'of/from', نژاد = 'lineage', سران = 'leaders/chiefs'); together it asserts noble or chiefly ancestry.
segments.s544-g17: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آن ماه‌رخ روی is a noun phrase in which ماه‌رخ (literally 'moon-faced') is an adjectival epithet modifying روی 'face'. شاپور is the agent/name; دید is the simple past 'saw' (3rd singular). Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal marker → demonstrative + epithet + head noun → subject → verb.
segments.s544-g18: بیامد is the past tense 'he came' (3rd singular) and functions as the main verb of this clause. برو here is a poetic/contracted particle (see note) modifying the motion—literally attested as a contraction of بر او/برِ او or a directional 'toward her' rather than an imperative 'go'. آفرین گسترید is a light-noun + verb phrase: آفرین 'praise/exclamation' as object and گسترید (past) 'spread, showered' as the verb (3rd person), i.e. 'he spread/poured forth praise.'
segments.s544-g19: که introduces the content/result of the preceding praise — here functioning like 'that' or 'may it be that'. شادان and خندان are predicative adjectives 'joyful' and 'smiling/cheerful'. بدی appears twice in an archaic/poetic optative or verbal use (a blessing-form) rather than the noun 'evil' listed in the sense table; in context it functions as an optative/old-form verbal ending meaning roughly 'may (you/it) be/make'. شاه sits between the two predicates as the person affected ('the king').
segments.s544-g20: همه‌ساله 'every year' sets the temporal frame. از بی‌گزندان is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of (or safe) unharmed' where بی‌گزندان (adjective/adv.) means 'free from harm'. The final بدی repeats the same archaic/poetic optative/verbal form as above (a blessing sense: 'may you be'/'may it remain'), not the nominal sense 'evil' in the provided list.
segments.s544-g21: کنون 'now' and بی‌گمان 'undoubtedly' are adverbial modifiers setting the speaker's assessment. تشنه باشد is a predicative phrase 'is/thirsty' where باشد is a modal/expletive form (classical Persian uses باشد to mark probability or a hortative/jussive nuance: 'must be/is likely to be'). ستور is the subject 'steed/beast' and follows the predicate here for poetic word order.
segments.s544-g22: This clause expresses purpose/result/movement. The tokenization here separates ب + دین + رود, but together they function like بدین ... رود 'therefore/with this ... goes' (بدین = 'by/with this' or 'therefore') and رود is the verb 'goes' (classical spelling رود for رَوَد). اندرون آب شور is a prepositional noun phrase 'into/within brackish water' (اندرون = interior/within; آب شور = salted/brackish water). Note: the listed senses for دین and رود in the table do not match their use here (hence -1).
segments.s544-g23: به چاه اندرون 'in/into the well' marks location. آب سردست و خوش is a nominal predicate describing that 'the water is cold (سردست) and pleasant (خوش)'. سردست here is an archaic/poetic adjective meaning something like 'very cold' or 'cool', not the modern senses 'ringleader' or 'wrist' listed in the table, so that entry is treated as non-matching.
segments.s544-g24: بفرمای here is a polite imperative/formula meaning 'please (say/do)' or 'be kind enough (to permit/command)'. تا introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'. من is the first-person pronoun 'I'. بوم is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'I may become / I will be' (not the noun 'homeland' in the provided senses), and آب‌کش here means 'water-drawer' (the person who draws water) rather than the listed senses (colander/sieve), so those two items are marked as non-matching.
segments.s544-g73: کنیزک بدو گفت is a straightforward S (کنیزک) + indirect object (بدو = 'to him') + verb (گفت = 'said') order; the speaker is 'the maidservant' and she 'said to him'. The following phrase کای شهریار is a vocative inserted after گفت: کای is the archaic/poetic vocative 'O' addressing شهریار ('king').
segments.s544-g74: هرانگه که introduces a subordinate temporal/conditional clause ('whenever/if'); یابم is first-person present-subjunctive/optative ('I may find / I should find' — used here as 'if/when I find' or 'if I obtain'). به جان is an oath-like prepositional phrase ('by my life' — به used adverbially), and زینهار is an admonitory interjection ('beware/take heed') finishing the quoted clause.
segments.s544-g75: بگویم here is a first-person verbal form meaning 'I will tell / I shall say' (in epic Persian it can have a hortative/subjunctive sense); the following همه پیش تو (‘everything/in full in your presence’) places the object and locative/preamble before the possessor phrase من نژاد. من precedes نژاد in an older/poetic possessive construction and functions as 'my', so من نژاد = 'my lineage'.
segments.s544-g76: چو (here = 'when/if') begins the temporal/conditional clause; یابم is again a first-person subjunctive/present ('I obtain / I am granted'); ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; خشم شهنشاه = 'the shah's anger'; داد in this context functions as a verbal past form 'gave' used elliptically to mean 'was granted/delivered' — together the phrase means 'when I am delivered/spared from the shah's wrath'. This line is slightly elliptical, a typical feature of epic diction.
segments.s544-g77: بدو گفت شاپور is an inverted word order common in poetry: indirect object (بدو = 'to him') + verb (گفت = 'said') + subject (شاپور = 'Shapur'), so 'Shapur said to him'. کز بوستان begins a relative/elliptical clause (کز = 'from/which'), linking to the following clause.
segments.s544-g78: نرست is the past form of رُستن; in this context the form with initial ن- is negative, so نرست = 'did not grow / did not spring up'. از چمن = 'from the meadow/garden'; کینه دوستان = 'hatred of friends' (کینه = 'hatred', دوستان = 'friends'). The clause therefore states that such hatred did not arise from the garden (i.e., it is not naturally grown there).
segments.s544-g79: بگوی is an imperative/hortative form meaning 'say (so) / tell (her/him)'; و = 'and'; ز من بیم در دل مدار is an instruction: ز من بیم = 'fear of me' (ز = 'of/from'); در دل = 'in the heart'; مدار is a negative/imperative verb here meaning 'do not keep / do not harbor' (classical poetic usage); so the whole segment means 'and do not keep fear of me in your heart.'
segments.s544-g80: نه introduces a negation applying to the following phrase 'از نامور دادگر شهریار' = 'from/of the famed just king'; از = 'from/of'; نامور = 'renowned/famed'; دادگر = 'just (ruler)'; شهریار = 'king'. Together this qualifies the previous clause: 'do not be afraid of the famed, just king.'

sentences.s544-s1: Now listen to the tale of Mehrak's daughter — Aba-gird Shapur, the swordsman.
sentences.s544-s2: When, for a short while, that age arose, the sovereign's fortune became radiant.
sentences.s544-s3: One morning at dawn the king went out to the hunt, and the wise Shapur accompanied him on the road.
sentences.s544-s4: Horsemen galloped in every direction; from the hunt they rode off into the plain.
sentences.s544-s29: Thus he answered: "I have heard this tale many times from the lips of the righteous."
sentences.s544-s30: that Shapur's wrist (arm) had the strength of an elephant and, in generosity, was like the Nile.
sentences.s544-s31: He is above the cypress and iron‑skinned, and in every respect resembles Bahman.
sentences.s544-s32: Shapur said to her, "O moon-faced one, whatever I ask you, speak truthfully."
sentences.s544-s17: The worshipper heard and ran; he seized the rope and put the heavy bucket on the wheel.
sentences.s544-s18: When the heavy, weighty bucket became full of water, the worshipper was thrown face‑first.
sentences.s544-s19: When the heavy bucket would not come up from the well, King Shapur quickly arrived.
sentences.s544-s20: He said to the worshipper, 'O half‑woman! This bucket is not for a woman; it (involves) many ropes.'
sentences.s544-s45: He said to them, “Give me that fair‑faced maiden, and let the heavens be witness for me.”
sentences.s544-s46: At his command the elder handed her over to him, according to the rites of his fire‑worshippers.
sentences.s544-s13: Shapur said to her, “O moon‑faced one, why have you been distressed by this conversation?”
sentences.s544-s14: That there be with me devoted men who will draw cold water from this well.
sentences.s544-s15: The young maid turned her face aside, withdrew (went away), and sat down by the stream.
sentences.s544-s16: The king ordered a devotee, “Bring the bucket and draw water from the well.”
sentences.s544-s5: From a distance there appeared a broad plain, full of gardens, fields, iwans and palaces.
sentences.s544-s6: Shapur kept riding (galloping) until he came before the village; he dismounted from the road at the inn of the great khan.
sentences.s544-s7: A certain place — a wide-spreading garden and a pleasant dwelling; a young man entered that green spot.
sentences.s544-s8: He saw a maiden like the moon; cast down from the heavens, tender‑hearted, she was by the well.
sentences.s544-s33: Reveal yourself so that I may know what your lineage is; on this face of yours, whose sign is it?
sentences.s544-s34: She said to him, 'I am the daughter of the chief; therefore I am thus fair and finely formed.'
sentences.s544-s35: She answered thus, that a lie never takes hold upon kings' splendour.
sentences.s544-s36: A farmer does not have a moon‑faced daughter with this face, this complexion and this scent.
sentences.s544-s41: The little maid said to him, "I am the daughter Mehrak, newly born."
sentences.s544-s42: Piety brought me wisdom; then he entrusted me to this cultured chief.
sentences.s544-s43: Because of fear of that famed king I thus became a menial and an attendant.
sentences.s544-s44: He came; Shapur advanced to his place, and the chief stood before him at his feet.
sentences.s544-s25: When the little maidservant lifted the bucket for him, she came forward and spread a loving praise.
sentences.s544-s26: May you never taste an evil draught; as long as time endures, may wisdom always be your teacher.
sentences.s544-s27: By the power of Shapur the king, Ardashir will become (king); undoubtedly, the water in the well will be milk.
sentences.s544-s28: The young man said to the smooth‑tongued girl, 'What do you know — that I am Shapur, O moon‑faced one?'
sentences.s544-s21: She kept drawing up several buckets of water from the well; you became full of suffering and calls for help.
sentences.s544-s22: A rope came and bound (tied) the attendant; that difficult task became grievous upon the despised king.
sentences.s544-s23: When the king saw the suffering caused by the heavy bucket, he lavished praise upon that fair‑faced one.
sentences.s544-s24: That the kind‑hearted one endured so heavy a thing — verily he is of the lineage of chiefs.
sentences.s544-s9: When Shapur saw that moon-faced visage, he came forward and showered her with praise.
sentences.s544-s10: May the king be joyful and all be smiling; may you be unhurt every year.
sentences.s544-s11: Now, surely the steed is thirsty; for this reason it goes into the brackish water there.
sentences.s544-s12: The water inside the well is cold and pleasant; please (say so) that I may become the water-drawer.
sentences.s544-s37: The maidservant said to him, "O king, whenever I find out — by my life, take heed!"
sentences.s544-s38: I will tell you my whole lineage before you, when I am delivered from the shah's wrath.
sentences.s544-s39: Shapur said to him, "That which did not grow from the garden was the hatred of friends."
sentences.s544-s40: Say (so), and do not harbor fear of me in your heart — do not fear the famed, just king.
